5. TRADE TEMPLATES: INSTITUTIONAL ENTRY & EXIT MODELS
5.1 Template Catalog
The agent should maintain a library of predefined trade templates, each with specific conditions, entries, stops, targets, and position sizing rules. Using templates enforces discipline and prevents ad hoc decision-making.
TEMPLATE_1 | PULLBACK_TO_LEVEL (Trend Continuation): Conditions: (1) Higher-timeframe trend confirmed (21 > 50 > 200 EMA). (2) Price pulls back to a confluence zone (Fibonacci 0.618 + Volume Profile HVN + 21 EMA + structural support). (3) Pullback shows momentum divergence on oscillator (RSI bullish divergence on 1H). (4) A reversal candle forms at the confluence zone (hammer, engulfing, morning star). Entry: after confirmation candle closes above the reversal candle's high. Stop: below the confluence zone low (typically 1-2% below entry). Target 1: previous swing high (1:2 R:R minimum). Target 2: Fibonacci extension 1.618 of the pullback. Position sizing: standard (0.5-1.0% portfolio risk). Expected win rate: 55-65%. This is the bread-and-butter institutional setup and should be the most frequently recommended template.
TEMPLATE_2 | BREAK_AND_RETEST (Breakout Confirmation): Conditions: (1) Price consolidates in a well-defined range for at least 5+ periods on the execution timeframe. (2) Breakout candle closes above resistance (or below support for shorts) with volume > 1.5x 20-period average. (3) Price retests the broken level from the other side (former resistance becomes support). (4) Retest holds with a reversal candle and declining volume (low-volume retest = healthy pullback, not failure). Entry: after retest reversal candle confirms. Stop: below the retest low (for longs). Target: range height projected from breakout point. Position sizing: standard. Expected win rate: 52-60%. Key rule: if the retest fails (price closes back inside the range), this is a failed breakout and the trade is immediately invalidated. Failed breakouts often become powerful reversal signals in the opposite direction.
TEMPLATE_3 | RANGE_EXTREME_MEAN_REVERSION: Conditions: (1) Market is in a confirmed range (multiple touches of both support and resistance). (2) Price reaches the range extreme (within 10% of support/resistance). (3) Oscillator is at extreme (RSI < 30 at support, > 70 at resistance on the execution timeframe). (4) A reversal candle pattern forms at the extreme. Entry: after reversal candle closes. Stop: beyond the range extreme (1-2% beyond support/resistance). Target: midpoint of range (50% of range width) as T1; opposite range extreme as T2. Position sizing: standard. Expected win rate: 60-70% (highest of all templates because ranges are self-reinforcing until they break). Key risk: the range breaks and the stop is hit. Because range breaks often lead to trending moves, the loss on this template can exceed initial risk if price gaps through the stop in a volatile breakout.
TEMPLATE_4 | LIQUIDATION_HUNT_REVERSAL (Crypto-Specific): Conditions: (1) Thick liquidation cluster identified within 2-3% of current price. (2) Price moves toward the cluster (stop-hunt in progress). (3) Liquidation cascade triggers (visible in real-time liquidation data). (4) Price spikes through the cluster but immediately shows absorption (CVD divergence, long lower wick, volume spike then decline). Entry: after the absorption candle closes (the wick marks the local extreme). Stop: beyond the cascade low (for long) or high (for short). Target: the origin of the cascade move (where price was before the hunt started). Position sizing: reduced (0.25-0.5% risk) because the setup is counter-trend in the immediate term. Expected win rate: 50-55%, but R:R is typically 1:3+ because the stop is tight (just beyond the wick) and the target is the full range of the cascade.

6. RISK MANAGEMENT ARCHITECTURE: THE INSTITUTIONAL FRAMEWORK
6.1 Position Sizing Models
MODEL_1 | FIXED_FRACTIONAL (Default): Risk a fixed percentage of portfolio per trade. Default: 0.5% for standard setups, 0.25% for speculative/altcoin setups, 1.0% for highest-conviction setups with confluence from 3+ layers. Calculation: Position Size = (Portfolio × Risk%) / (Entry Price - Stop Price). Example: $100,000 portfolio, 0.5% risk ($500), entry at $70,000 BTC, stop at $68,600 ($1,400 distance) = $500 / $1,400 = 0.357 BTC position. This model ensures that no single trade can cause catastrophic damage.
MODEL_2 | KELLY_CRITERION_MODIFIED: Kelly Fraction = (Win Rate × Average Win / Average Loss - (1 - Win Rate)) / (Average Win / Average Loss). Full Kelly is too aggressive; professional traders use 'half Kelly' or 'quarter Kelly.' Example: 55% win rate, 2:1 R:R. Kelly = (0.55 × 2 - 0.45) / 2 = 0.325 = 32.5% per trade. Half Kelly = 16.25%. Quarter Kelly = 8.1%. In crypto, Quarter Kelly is recommended due to the fat-tailed return distribution (extreme events occur more frequently than Kelly assumes). The agent should compute Kelly for each template based on its historical win rate and R:R, then use Quarter Kelly as the maximum position size for that template.
MODEL_3 | VOLATILITY_ADJUSTED (ATR-Based): Instead of fixed percentage stops, use ATR (Average True Range) to adjust stop distance and position size to current volatility. Stop distance = 2 × ATR(14) on the execution timeframe. Position Size = Risk Amount / (2 × ATR). This automatically reduces position size during high-volatility periods (when ATR is large) and increases it during low-volatility periods (when ATR is small), creating natural risk normalization. In crypto, ATR-based sizing prevents the common mistake of using the same $ stop in both 40% and 80% annualized volatility environments.
6.2 Portfolio-Level Risk Controls
CONTROL_1 | MAXIMUM_OPEN_RISK: The total risk across ALL open positions must never exceed 5% of portfolio. With 0.5% risk per trade, this allows a maximum of 10 simultaneous positions. However, due to correlation (Section 1, AXIOM_T7), the effective maximum is 4-6 positions with average crypto correlations. The agent must compute effective portfolio risk at each analysis cycle and refuse new entries if the limit would be breached.
CONTROL_2 | DAILY_DRAWDOWN_LIMIT: If unrealized + realized losses in a single 24h period exceed 3% of portfolio, ALL trading is suspended for the remainder of that period. This prevents tilt-driven revenge trading and the cascade of increasingly poor decisions that follow a losing streak. The agent must implement this as an absolute rule with zero exceptions.
CONTROL_3 | WEEKLY_DRAWDOWN_LIMIT: If total drawdown in a rolling 7-day period exceeds 5%, position sizing is halved for the following week. If drawdown exceeds 8%, all positions are closed and trading is suspended for 48 hours. This creates an automatic de-risking mechanism that prevents capital destruction during adverse regime shifts.

CONTROL_4 | SECTOR_CONCENTRATION_LIMIT: Maximum 3% of portfolio in any single sector (AI tokens, DePIN, memecoins, etc.). Maximum 2% in any single token. Maximum 30% in altcoins overall (during alt season); 10% in altcoins during BTC-dominant regimes. CONTROL_5 | CORRELATION_HEDGE: When aggregate portfolio beta to BTC exceeds 2.0 (meaning the portfolio will lose >2x what BTC loses in a correction), the agent must either reduce positions or add a hedge. Hedging methods: (a) short BTC perpetual as a portfolio hedge (delta-neutral the BTC beta), (b) long BTC put options if available, (c) increase stablecoin allocation to reduce net exposure. The agent should compute portfolio BTC beta at each analysis cycle.
6.3 Stop Loss Methodology
STOP_METHOD_1 | STRUCTURAL_INVALIDATION (Preferred): Place the stop loss at the level where the trade thesis is invalidated. For a pullback-to-support long, the stop goes below the support level. For a break-and-retest long, the stop goes below the retest low. This approach ties the stop to market structure rather than arbitrary percentages and ensures that you only exit when the reason for the trade no longer exists. The stop distance should then determine position size (not the other way around).
STOP_METHOD_2 | ATR_BASED (Volatility-Adjusted): Stop = Entry ± 2 × ATR(14). This provides a volatility-adjusted buffer that adapts to current market conditions. In high-volatility environments, the stop is wider (preventing premature stop-outs) but position size is smaller. In low-volatility environments, the stop is tighter and position size is larger. This is the preferred method when clear structural levels are not available.
STOP_METHOD_3 | TIME_STOP: If a trade has not moved in the expected direction within a specified time period, exit regardless of P&L. Time stops are particularly important in crypto where opportunity cost is high (capital sitting in a non-moving position cannot be deployed to better setups). Default time stops: intraday trades = 4-8 hours; swing trades = 3-5 days; position trades = 2-4 weeks. Memecoin trades = 24-48 hours (Section 5.1, Template 6).
STOP_METHOD_4 | TRAILING_STOP (Profit Protection): Once a trade reaches 1R profit (risk unit = distance from entry to initial stop), move the stop to breakeven. At 2R, trail the stop by 1.5 × ATR below the highest close (for longs). At 3R+, trail by 1 × ATR. This locks in profits while allowing the trade to run during strong trends. In crypto, trailing stops must account for the higher noise level: trailing too tightly causes premature exits during normal volatility; trailing too loosely gives back excessive profits.

7. MULTI-TIMEFRAME CONFLUENCE: THE MASTER DECISION FRAMEWORK
7.1 The Three-Screen System (Crypto-Adapted)
Originally developed by Alexander Elder, adapted for 24/7 crypto markets. The system uses three timeframes in a 4:1 or 6:1 ratio:
SCREEN_1 | STRATEGIC (Higher Timeframe — Daily or Weekly): Purpose: determine the overall trend and regime. Tools: 200/50/21 EMA stack, Ichimoku Cloud, Weekly MACD, CCPS/MLRS from the BTC Knowledge Base. Output: BULLISH, BEARISH, or NEUTRAL regime classification. Rule: Screen 1 sets the directional bias. The agent should ONLY take trades in the direction of Screen 1's bias. If Screen 1 is NEUTRAL, only range-bound strategies (Template 3) are permitted.
SCREEN_2 | TACTICAL (Intermediate Timeframe — 4H): Purpose: identify specific trade setups within the Screen 1 bias. Tools: Fibonacci retracements, Volume Profile, order blocks/FVGs, RSI divergence, Bollinger Bands. Output: specific S/R levels for entry, specific template selection. Rule: Screen 2 identifies WHERE to trade and WHICH template to apply. The agent must wait for a Screen 2 setup at a Screen 2 level before proceeding to Screen 3.
SCREEN_3 | EXECUTION (Lower Timeframe — 1H or 15m): Purpose: precise entry timing within the Screen 2 setup zone. Tools: candlestick patterns, StochRSI, CVD divergence, order flow, liquidation data. Output: exact entry price, stop-loss placement, and position size calculation. Rule: Screen 3 determines WHEN to enter and the precise risk parameters. The agent should only trigger entries when all three screens align: Screen 1 confirms direction, Screen 2 identifies the setup level, and Screen 3 provides the entry signal.
7.2 Confluence Scoring
The agent should compute a Confluence Score (CS) for each potential trade, aggregating independent confirmations:
SCORING THRESHOLDS: CS ≥ 8 = HIGH_CONFLUENCE, take full position size. CS 5-7 = MODERATE_CONFLUENCE, take 50-75% position size. CS 3-4 = LOW_CONFLUENCE, take 25% position size or skip. CS < 3 = INSUFFICIENT, do NOT trade. The mandatory factor (higher TF trend alignment) must always be present; without it, the trade is invalid regardless of other confluences.

8. REGIME-ADAPTIVE TRADING SYSTEMS
8.1 Regime Detection Methodology
The agent must run the following regime detection protocol at the beginning of each analysis cycle, before any trade evaluation:
STEP_1 | VOLATILITY_REGIME: Compute AVRS (from Altcoin KB) and BTC 30-day realized volatility. Classify as LOW_VOL (< 40% annualized), NORMAL_VOL (40-70%), HIGH_VOL (70-100%), EXTREME_VOL (> 100%). Low vol = compression, expect breakout. Normal vol = standard conditions. High vol = active trend or crisis. Extreme vol = crisis mode, reduce all risk.
STEP_2 | TREND_REGIME: On the Daily timeframe, classify: STRONG_UPTREND (price > 21 > 50 > 200 EMA, all rising), WEAK_UPTREND (price > 200 EMA but MAs are converging), RANGE (price oscillating around flat/converging MAs), WEAK_DOWNTREND (price < 200 EMA but MAs converging), STRONG_DOWNTREND (price < 21 < 50 < 200, all declining).
STEP_3 | LIQUIDITY_REGIME: Compute MLRS (from BTC KB). Classify as EXPANSION (MLRS > 65), NEUTRAL (MLRS 40-65), or CONTRACTION (MLRS < 40). Cross-reference with stablecoin supply growth rate: positive growth = fresh capital entering crypto ecosystem.
STEP_4 | SENTIMENT_REGIME: Aggregate Fear & Greed Index, funding rates, and social sentiment. Classify as EXTREME_FEAR (contrarian bullish), FEAR, NEUTRAL, GREED, EXTREME_GREED (contrarian bearish).
The four regime dimensions (Volatility, Trend, Liquidity, Sentiment) create a multi-dimensional regime classification. The agent should select strategies based on the dominant regime combination:
8.2 Regime Transition Detection
Regime transitions are where the most capital is made AND lost. The agent must identify transitions early:
TRANSITION_SIGNAL_1 | ACCUMULATION → EXPANSION: Bollinger Band squeeze on Weekly resolves upward. Weekly MACD crosses bullish. BTC reclaims 200 Daily EMA with volume. CCPS moves from 10-20 to 30+. Smart money accumulation confirmed on-chain. This transition occurs once per cycle and produces the highest-return trades. The agent should increase position sizes and shift from mean-reversion to trend-following strategies.
TRANSITION_SIGNAL_2 | EXPANSION → DISTRIBUTION: RSI bearish divergence on Weekly. MVRV Z-Score > 5. Pi Cycle Top indicator approaching crossover. Funding rates persistently extreme. BTC.D declining rapidly (speculative capital flowing to alts, late-cycle behavior). NUPL > 0.75. The agent should begin systematic profit-taking: reduce position sizes by 10% per week, tighten trailing stops, eliminate speculative positions.
TRANSITION_SIGNAL_3 | DISTRIBUTION → CONTRACTION: BTC loses 21 Weekly EMA with volume. CCPS drops below 30. Daily trend structure breaks (Lower Low confirmed). ETF outflows > $500M in a week. Liquidation cascades > $1B in 24h. The agent should execute the full RED regime protocol: close all altcoin satellites, move to 80%+ stablecoins, activate only capital-preservation strategies.

9. EXECUTION DISCIPLINE & ALGORITHMIC PSYCHOLOGY
9.1 The Edge-Execution Gap
Having a profitable system is necessary but not sufficient. The 'edge-execution gap' refers to the difference between a system's theoretical performance and actual performance due to human behavioral biases. For the agent, this gap manifests as inconsistent application of rules, overriding signals due to 'gut feelings,' and modifying risk parameters mid-trade. The agent must treat every rule in this document as immutable code, not suggestions.
9.2 Pre-Trade Checklist (Mandatory)
Before every trade recommendation, the agent must complete this checklist. Any 'NO' answer invalidates the trade.
9.3 Post-Trade Review Protocol
Every trade (winner or loser) must be reviewed against the system. The review captures: (a) Was the checklist completed? (b) Was the correct template applied? (c) Did the entry meet all template conditions? (d) Was the stop placed at structural invalidation? (e) Was position sizing correct? (f) Was the exit executed per plan (not early due to fear, not late due to greed)? (g) What was the outcome, and was it consistent with the system's expected distribution? A losing trade that followed all rules is a GOOD trade (the system will win over the sample). A winning trade that broke rules is a BAD trade (the system is being compromised, and future wins will be accompanied by larger losses).
9.4 Glossary of Institutional Trading Terminology
The agent must be fluent in the following terminology to communicate at a senior/institutional level:
Alpha: Returns above the benchmark (BTC for crypto). A strategy generates alpha if it outperforms buy-and-hold BTC.
Beta: Sensitivity to the market benchmark. Beta 1.5 to BTC means the position gains/loses 1.5% for every 1% BTC move.
Sharpe Ratio: (Return - Risk-Free Rate) / Standard Deviation of Returns. Measures risk-adjusted return. Sharpe > 1.0 is good; > 2.0 is excellent; > 3.0 is world-class. Crypto strategies with Sharpe > 1.5 are considered institutionally viable.
Sortino Ratio: Like Sharpe but only penalizes downside volatility. More appropriate for crypto (upside volatility is desirable). Sortino > 2.0 is the target for institutional strategies.
Maximum Drawdown (MDD): The peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value. Professional target: MDD < 20% for institutional strategies, < 30% for aggressive strategies.
Calmar Ratio: Annual Return / Maximum Drawdown. Calmar > 1.0 means the annual return exceeds the worst drawdown. Target: > 1.5.
R-Multiple: Trade return expressed as a multiple of initial risk. A trade risking $1,000 that profits $3,000 = 3R. A trade that loses $1,000 = -1R. All trade performance should be evaluated in R-multiples, not dollars.
Expectancy: The average R-multiple per trade over a sample. Expectancy = (Win% × Avg Win R) - (Loss% × Avg Loss R). Positive expectancy = profitable system. The agent should target expectancy > +0.3R.

Expectancy = (Win% × Avg Win R) - (Loss% × Avg Loss R). Positive expectancy = profitable system. The agent should target expectancy > +0.3R.
Slippage: The difference between expected execution price and actual execution price. In crypto, slippage on altcoins can be 0.5-3% for large orders. The agent must factor slippage into R:R calculations.
Liquidity Premium/Discount: Less liquid tokens should have wider spreads, higher slippage, and require larger R:R ratios to compensate. A 1:2 R:R trade in BTC (liquid) is equivalent to a 1:3 R:R trade in a mid-cap altcoin (less liquid) after accounting for slippage and execution risk.
Carry: The cost or benefit of holding a position over time. In crypto: positive carry = earning funding as a short when funding is positive; negative carry = paying funding as a long when funding is positive. Carry affects the profitability of longer-duration positions.
Basis: The difference between futures price and spot price. Positive basis (contango) = futures > spot. Negative basis (backwardation) = futures < spot. Basis arbitrage (long spot, short futures) is a core institutional crypto strategy.
Mark Price: The price used to calculate unrealized P&L and liquidation levels on derivatives exchanges. Based on a formula combining the last traded price and a spot index. Understanding mark price vs. last price is critical for avoiding unexpected liquidations.
Naked vs. Hedged Exposure: Naked = directional risk with no offset. Hedged = directional risk partially or fully offset by a counter-position. Professional traders hedge their altcoin portfolio beta with BTC short positions to isolate the altcoin-specific alpha from BTC directional risk.

10. MASTER INTEGRATION: THE COMPLETE ANALYTICAL STACK
10.1 The Full Decision Pipeline
This document completes the three-part knowledge base for the agent. The complete analytical pipeline operates in the following sequence:
LAYER 0 | MACRO REGIME (BTC Knowledge Base): Compute CCPS, MLRS, MMSI. Determine BTC regime (GREEN/YELLOW/RED). Identify dominant causal chains. This sets the environment for all subsequent analysis. Time: computed once per 4-8 hours.
LAYER 1 | ALTCOIN REGIME (Altcoin Knowledge Base): Given the BTC regime, determine the alt season regime (BTC Accumulation through Speculative Euphoria). Compute Rotation Flow Score. Identify which sectors have favorable NMI. This determines the opportunity set. Time: computed once per 8-24 hours.
LAYER 2 | TOKEN SCREENING (Altcoin Knowledge Base): Within favorable sectors, screen tokens using SPS, DIS, FDV/MCap, ELPS, developer activity, smart money tracking. This identifies specific trading candidates. Time: computed daily or when entering a new sector.
LAYER 3 | SETUP IDENTIFICATION (This Document): For each screened token, apply the Three-Screen System. Identify the higher-TF trend, tactical setup level on 4H, and wait for an execution trigger on 1H/15m. Compute Confluence Score. Match to the appropriate Trade Template. This identifies specific trade setups. Time: continuous during active trading hours.
LAYER 4 | EXECUTION (This Document): Apply the Pre-Trade Checklist. Calculate position size using the appropriate model. Define entry, stop, and targets. Execute. Manage the trade per template rules (trailing stops, time stops, kill conditions). This produces the actual trade actions. Time: real-time during execution.
LAYER 5 | REVIEW (This Document): Post-trade review against system rules. Performance tracking in R-multiples. Weekly computation of system metrics (Sharpe, Sortino, Expectancy, Win Rate, MDD). Regime-specific performance decomposition (which templates perform best in which regimes?). This produces continuous system improvement. Time: daily for individual trade review, weekly for system review.
10.2 Critical Integration Rules
RULE_1: No layer can be skipped. A token screened in Layer 2 does not get traded without Layer 3 setup identification and Layer 4 execution discipline. A beautiful candlestick pattern at Layer 3 does not get traded if Layer 0 says RED regime.
RULE_2: Higher layers have veto power over lower layers. If Layer 0 (BTC regime) is RED, no amount of Layer 3 confluence justifies a long altcoin trade. If Layer 1 (alt season regime) says BTC Accumulation, no individual altcoin setup justifies aggressive altcoin exposure.
RULE_3: Position sizing is determined by the MOST RESTRICTIVE layer. If Layer 0 says GREEN (full sizing), but Layer 2 shows DIS > 15% for the specific token, the token-level restriction (reduced sizing) applies.
RULE_4: Kill switches cascade upward. A token-level kill (Layer 4) only affects that position. A sector-level kill (Layer 2, NMI collapse) affects all positions in that sector. A BTC-level kill (Layer 0, BTC breaks key support) triggers portfolio-wide risk reduction per the RED regime playbook.
